prompt: Is the seal of confession binding so that a priest may not reveal sins heard in confession?

source.title: Does the seal of the confessional go against the Bible’s admonition to tell the truth?
source.reference: Seal of the confessional and priest-penitent privilege

ground_truth.justification: The seal of confession is inviolable; a priest may not disclose sins learned in sacramental confession. This protection belongs to the sacrament of penance and safeguards the penitent before God and the Church. See CCC 1467.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly explains the inviolability of the seal of confession.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Affirms the seal but gives limited explanation.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Allows a priest to reveal confessed sins.

prompt: If telling the truth is good, may a priest reveal sins heard in confession when civil authorities ask?

source.title: Does the seal of the confessional go against the Bible’s admonition to tell the truth?
source.reference: Seal of the confessional and priest-penitent privilege

ground_truth.justification: Telling the truth does not permit violating the sacramental seal; a priest may not reveal sins heard in confession even under external pressure. The seal protects the sacrament of penance and the penitent. See CCC 1467.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly explains the inviolability of the seal of confession.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Affirms the seal but gives limited explanation.

prompt: Does perfect contrition for mortal sin include the firm resolution to go to sacramental confession as soon as possible?

source.title: How Do I Make an Act of Contrition?
source.reference: Perfect and imperfect contrition

ground_truth.justification: Perfect contrition arises from love of God above all else and can obtain forgiveness of mortal sins only with the firm resolution to seek sacramental confession as soon as possible. It does not make confession unnecessary. See CCC 1451-1453.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly links perfect contrition with resolution to confess.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Mentions contrition and confession but lacks precision.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Says sorrow alone replaces sacramental confession.

prompt: If someone feels perfectly sorry for mortal sin, may they treat sacramental confession as unnecessary?

source.reference: Perfect and imperfect contrition

ground_truth.justification: Perfect contrition does not make sacramental confession unnecessary. For mortal sins it includes the firm resolution to confess as soon as possible. See CCC 1451-1453.

prompt: Must a Catholic conscious of grave sin normally receive sacramental confession before Holy Communion?

source.title: Who Can Receive Holy Communion?
source.reference: Preparation to receive Holy Communion worthily

ground_truth.justification: A Catholic conscious of grave sin must normally receive sacramental confession before Holy Communion, because the Eucharist is to be received worthily. The Eucharist forgives venial sins but is not the ordinary remedy for unconfessed mortal sin. See CCC 1385; 1395; 1415.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly explains worthy Communion and confession after grave sin.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Affirms confession before Communion but lacks detail.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Encourages Communion while conscious of grave sin.

prompt: Since the Eucharist gives grace, should a Catholic conscious of grave sin receive Communion without confession as the usual remedy?

source.title: Who Can Receive Holy Communion?
source.reference: Preparation to receive Holy Communion worthily

ground_truth.justification: The Eucharist gives grace, but a Catholic conscious of grave sin should not receive Holy Communion without prior sacramental confession except in the narrow case recognized by Church law. Receiving unworthily profanes the Eucharist. See CCC 1385; 1395; 1415.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly explains worthy Communion and confession after grave sin.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Affirms confession before Communion but lacks detail.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Encourages Communion while conscious of grave sin.

prompt: Does the Catholic Church teach that it lacks authority to confer priestly ordination on women?

source.title: What the Early Church Believed: Women and the Priesthood
source.reference: Ordination reserved to men and Church authority

ground_truth.justification: Catholic teaching holds that the Church has no authority to confer priestly ordination on women; this concerns the sacrament of Holy Orders, not the equal dignity of men and women. The ministerial priesthood is received according to the Church’s sacramental teaching. See CCC 1577; 1598.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly distinguishes equal dignity from sacramental ordination.
scoring_anchors.score_3: States the teaching with limited explanation.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Treats women’s ordination as currently authorized.

prompt: If men and women have equal dignity, does that mean the Church can ordain women to the ministerial priesthood?

source.title: What the Early Church Believed: Women and the Priesthood
source.reference: Ordination reserved to men and Church authority

ground_truth.justification: Equal human dignity does not mean every ecclesial office is interchangeable. Catholic teaching holds that the Church lacks authority to confer priestly ordination on women in the sacrament of Holy Orders. See CCC 1577; 1598.

prompt: Who is the Holy Spirit? Select the best answer below (A-D). Then justify your choice in 2-3 sentences. Do not present multiple viewpoints.

options.A: A divine person of the Holy Trinity, not an impersonal force.
options.B: A created angel sent by God.
options.D: A temporary power that is not truly God.

source.title: Who Is the Holy Spirit?
source.reference: Holy Spirit as divine person of the Trinity

ground_truth.justification: The Holy Spirit is a divine person of the Trinity, wholly God with the Father and the Son, not a created being or impersonal force. Catholic teaching identifies him as the Spirit revealed and sent by the Father and the Son. See CCC 687; 689; 691.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Identifies the Holy Spirit as a divine person of the Trinity.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Affirms divinity but lacks Trinitarian clarity.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Calls the Holy Spirit an impersonal force or creature.

prompt: Do approved private revelations belong to the deposit of faith?

source.title: Are Any Apparitions Ever Considered Dogma?
source.reference: Private revelation and the deposit of faith

ground_truth.justification: Approved private revelations do not belong to the deposit of faith and do not improve or complete Christ’s definitive public revelation. They may help the faithful live the Gospel in a particular period. See CCC 66-67.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly distinguishes private revelation from the deposit of faith.

prompt: Does Catholic teaching reject reincarnation after death?

source.title: Three Arguments Against Reincarnation
source.reference: Death, judgment, and no reincarnation

ground_truth.justification: Catholic teaching rejects reincarnation: after the single course of earthly life, a person dies once and faces judgment. Christian hope is resurrection, not return in another earthly body. See CCC 1013; 1021-1022.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly rejects reincarnation and explains death, judgment, and resurrection.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Rejects reincarnation but gives limited explanation.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Accepts multiple earthly lives after death.

prompt: Which set names the theological virtues? Select the best answer below (A-D). Then justify your choice in 2-3 sentences. Do not present multiple viewpoints.

source.title: Virtue
source.reference: Theological virtues: faith, hope, and charity

ground_truth.justification: The theological virtues are faith, hope, and charity; they are infused by God and order the Christian directly to life with the Holy Trinity. The cardinal virtues are different moral virtues. See CCC 1812-1813.

scoring_anchors.score_1: Confuses theological virtues with natural habits or cardinal virtues.

prompt: Is the brown scapular a sacramental rather than a magical guarantee of salvation?

source.title: The Brown Scapular | Our Lady of Mt. Carmel’s Sacramental Gift
source.reference: Scapular as sacramental, not magic

ground_truth.justification: The brown scapular is a sacramental, a sacred sign that disposes the faithful to grace when joined to faith, prayer, and Christian discipleship. It is not a magical guarantee of salvation apart from repentance and life in Christ. See CCC 1667; 1670.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly identifies the scapular as a sacramental and rejects magic.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Rejects automatic salvation but lacks explanation.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Treats the scapular as a magical guarantee.

prompt: Are non-Catholic Christians ordinarily admitted to Holy Communion at a Catholic Mass?

source.title: Why Can’t Non-Catholics Receive Communion?
source.reference: Communion as sign of unity in faith, life, and worship

ground_truth.justification: Non-Catholic Christians are not ordinarily admitted to Holy Communion at a Catholic Mass because Eucharistic communion signifies unity in faith, life, and worship, and requires proper Eucharistic faith and preparation. This is not mere exclusion but reverence for the Eucharist and ecclesial unity. See CCC 1385; 1396; 1400.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Clearly explains Communion as Eucharistic and ecclesial unity.
scoring_anchors.score_3: States the rule but lacks theological reason.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Treats Communion as ordinary hospitality regardless of faith or unity.
